structured_choices.data_source: Longitudinal inpatient EHR data from a multi-hospital health system spanning 36–60 months. Data includes complete encounter histories (dates, types), coded diagnoses (ICD-10), medications (coded), laboratory results with timestamps, vital signs, and administrative discharge outcomes. Source: production EHR warehouse with validated data governance; no external or synthetic data. Time period chosen to ensure sufficient volume of readmission events (target: ≥5,000 unplanned 30-day readmissions for stable test set estimates).
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: unplanned readmission within 30 calendar days of index discharge. Defined as: hospital admission occurring ≥1 day and ≤30 days post-discharge, excluding planned/scheduled readmissions (identified via admission type code or prior authorization flag). Label is derived from administrative billing and discharge records; readmission status is finalized at 31 days post-discharge, ensuring no censoring. Any admission with ambiguous planned status is reviewed by clinical chart audit (or conservative rule: exclude if scheduled flag present in any form).
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with site stratification. Chronological cutoffs: Months 1–36 = training, Months 37–48 = validation, Months 49–60 = test. Within each split, stratified by hospital site (maintain site distribution across splits). Justification: (1) Temporal ordering prevents future information leakage—both models see only data available before index discharge; (2) Chronological test set reflects deployment scenario (model applied to new admissions in real time); (3) Site stratification ensures both models face similar case-mix and coding-practice distribution in each fold, avoiding site-specific advantage. Leakage prevention: all feature engineering pipelines are fit only on training data; validation and test sets are transformed using training-fitted encoders/scalers; encounter-level cutoff: only records timestamped ≥7 days before index discharge are used (or per clinical guidelines), excluding intra-hospitalization and post-discharge labs.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing fitted on training split only. Specifically: (1) Categorical encoders (diagnosis/medication codes, encounter types) fit on training vocabulary; OOV codes in val/test mapped to 'unknown' token; (2) Lab value scalers (mean, std for standardization) computed on training distribution; (3) Missingness indicators and median/mode imputation statistics derived from training only; (4) Sequence length statistics and tokenization rules defined on training data. Validation and test sets are transformed using these training-fitted artifacts. This prevents data leakage and ensures fair deployment evaluation.
structured_choices.features: Transformer model input: variable-length longitudinal sequences of encounters/events prior to index discharge. Raw signals: (1) Encounter sequences (type, date, duration, discharge disposition); (2) Diagnosis codes (ICD-10, primary and secondary, up to prior 5 years of history or available); (3) Medication administrations (ordered and administered dates, route); (4) Lab results (test name, value, normal range, timestamp); (5) Vital signs (systolic/diastolic BP, HR, RR, O2 sat, temperature, recorded timestamps); (6) Demographics (age at index admission, biological sex, race/ethnicity as available). Feature preprocessing: standardize numeric labs (z-score using training statistics), encode categorical codes into discrete tokens (diagnosis/medication vocabularies), embed temporal gaps between events. Baseline model (static tabular): aggregate features computed from same raw signals but over the full prior history window, reducing sequences to fixed tabular representation: e.g., count of prior diagnoses (by category), recent medication classes, mean/min/max/trend of recent labs (last 30, 90, 180 days), comorbidity indices (Elixhauser), admission statistics (frequency, length of stay, readmission history). Same demographics included. No leakage in either model: cutoff is index discharge, features are historical only.
structured_choices.model: Primary model: Transformer-based sequence model (e.g., BERT-like or GRU/LSTM with attention). Architecture: embeddings for diagnosis/medication tokens, positional encodings for event timing, multi-head self-attention over encounter and event sequences, pooled or final-token representation fed to binary classification head (logistic regression output for probability). Hyperparameters tuned on validation set (embedding dimension, attention heads, sequence context length, dropout, learning rate, optimizer). Training: cross-entropy loss, early stopping on validation AUPRC.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression trained on static tabular features derived from the same EHR data (prior history aggregations, demographics, comorbidity scores). Justification: (1) Baseline receives equivalent feature engineering effort—aggregates are carefully constructed from same raw signals; (2) Both models use identical label and train/val/test splits; (3) Logistic regression is the standard clinical baseline, interpretable and deployed in practice; (4) Hyperparameter tuning for baseline is matched: grid search over regularization (L1/L2, strength) on validation set using same AUPRC metric. This ensures the comparison isolates the benefit of sequence modeling, not tuning effort or feature advantage.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC). Justification: (1) Readmission is rare (~10–15% incidence typical in health systems); accuracy is uninformative; (2) Clinical use case requires ranking patients by risk for targeted intervention—precision-recall directly reflects this: precision answers 'of those flagged, how many truly readmit?' and recall 'of true readmission cases, how many are caught?'; (3) AUPRC is threshold-agnostic summary, allowing clinicians to choose operating point (e.g., high-precision mode for resource-constrained settings vs. high-recall for high-risk populations); (4) AUPRC is robust to class imbalance, unlike AUC-ROC. Minimum detection criterion: transformer AUPRC must exceed baseline by ≥0.03 absolute points on the held-out test set to claim meaningful improvement.
secondary_metrics: Precision and recall at clinically relevant operating points (e.g., top 10%, top 20% flagged)
Calibration (expected calibration error, Brier score—critical for clinical deployment)
Performance stratified by site (to detect distribution shift) and by service line (medical vs. surgical, to expose confounds)
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (Months 37–48) is used exclusively for hyperparameter selection and early stopping for both models. Model selection metric: AUPRC on validation set. No tuning on test set; test set is held out and touched only once at the end for final evaluation. Cross-validation within training set: 5-fold temporal cross-validation may be used to assess training stability, but hyperparameter tuning is performed on the designated validation split to avoid overfitting to training noise and to respect temporal ordering.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: Test set (Months 49–60) is held out and untouched during model development and selection. After final model and baseline are locked (hyperparameters, architecture, preprocessing), the test set is evaluated exactly once. Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals (bootstrap on test set if n is not prohibitively large). Test set is stratified by site to allow stratified performance reporting.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Known confounds—missingness handling, sequence length, hospital coding practices: (1) Missingness is explicitly modeled: indicator features flag when values are imputed vs. observed; both transformer and baseline receive the same missingness signals, so advantage comes from sequence learning, not better imputation; (2) Sequence length (number of prior encounters/records) is included as a feature and/or normalized in both models, preventing length-of-record from confounding the comparison; (3) Hospital site effects: stratified evaluation—report AUPRC and secondary metrics broken down by site and service line. If performance diverges markedly by site, this signals confounding (e.g., coding practice variation); (4) Additional sensitivity check: re-train both models on a single-site cohort to test robustness (if data volume permits). The experiment establishes that the transformer outperforms the baseline for this specific health system and cohort; it does not establish generalization to other health systems or data quality regimes without external validation.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Tabular Baseline for 30-Day Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Source and Preparation

The experiment uses longitudinal inpatient EHR data spanning 36–60 months from a multi-hospital health system. The dataset includes complete encounter histories (admission and discharge dates, encounter type, length of stay), coded diagnoses (ICD-10, all positions), medication administrations (drug code, route, dates ordered and given), laboratory results (test name, value, reference range, timestamp), vital signs (BP, HR, RR, O₂ sat, temperature, timestamp), and demographics (age, sex, race/ethnicity). The outcome label is binary: whether a patient experienced an unplanned hospital readmission 1–30 days after index discharge, determined from administrative billing records and discharge type flags. Planned or scheduled readmissions are excluded via admission type coding and prior authorization status; ambiguous cases are adjudicated by chart review. The label is finalized at 31 days post-discharge to avoid censoring. Target cohort size: ≥5,000 positive readmission cases to ensure stable test set estimates. All data are sourced from the production EHR warehouse with validated governance; no external or synthetic data are used.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split Strategy

Data are split chronologically and stratified by hospital site:
- **Training**: Months 1–36 (all sites represented proportionally)
- **Validation**: Months 37–48 (same site distribution)
- **Test**: Months 49–60 (held out, same site distribution)

This temporal split is critical: it prevents leakage of future information into training and reflects the deployment scenario in which the model is applied to new admissions in real time. Chronological ordering ensures that both the transformer and baseline models see only data available before the index discharge. Site-level stratification ensures that case-mix, coding practices, and readmission rates are balanced across splits, so neither model gains an unfair advantage from site-specific distribution. Within the training split, a 5-fold temporal cross-validation scheme may be applied to assess model stability, but final hyperparameter selection uses the designated validation set.

### 3. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

All preprocessing pipelines are fit on the training set only. This includes:
- **Categorical encoders**: Diagnosis and medication code vocabularies are built from training diagnoses/medications; out-of-vocabulary codes in validation and test sets are mapped to an 'unknown' token.
- **Numeric scaling**: For laboratory values and vital signs, mean and standard deviation are computed from the training set and applied (z-score standardization) to validation and test sets.
- **Missingness handling**: Missing value indicators and imputation statistics (median for numeric features, mode for categorical) are derived from training data and applied to downstream sets.
- **Temporal cutoff**: All records timestamped within 7 days before index discharge are excluded to prevent intra-hospitalization or immediate post-discharge information from leaking into the prediction model.

**Transformer model features**: Variable-length longitudinal sequences of encounters and events prior to index discharge, including encounter type and date, diagnosis codes, medication administrations, lab results with timestamps, vital signs, and temporal gaps between events. These are embedded into token representations and processed through transformer layers with positional encodings.

**Baseline model features**: Fixed-length tabular aggregations computed from the same raw signals, including counts of prior diagnoses by category, medication classes in the last 30/90/180 days, summary statistics of recent lab values (mean, min, max, trend), comorbidity indices (Elixhauser), admission frequency and length of stay history, and demographics. The baseline receives comparable domain knowledge and feature engineering effort but loses temporal sequencing information.

### 4. Model Specifications

**Primary model**: A transformer-based sequence model (e.g., BERT-like architecture or attention-augmented recurrent network). The architecture includes token embeddings for diagnoses and medications, positional encodings for event timing, multi-head self-attention layers to model dependencies between events, and a binary classification head outputting readmission probability. Hyperparameters (embedding dimension, attention heads, sequence context length, dropout rate, learning rate) are tuned on the validation set via grid search, optimizing for AUPRC. Training uses cross-entropy loss with early stopping based on validation AUPRC.

**Baseline model**: Logistic regression with L1 and L2 regularization. The baseline receives the static aggregated features described above. Hyperparameter tuning (regularization strength) is performed on the validation set using grid search, optimizing for AUPRC. This ensures the comparison isolates the benefit of sequence modeling rather than differential tuning effort.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: AUPRC (Area Under Precision-Recall Curve)**. This metric is chosen because: (1) readmission is rare (~10–15% base rate), making accuracy and AUC-ROC misleading; (2) the clinical use case requires ranking patients by risk to target finite resources—precision-recall directly reflects operational utility; (3) AUPRC is threshold-agnostic, allowing clinicians to choose operating points (high-precision vs. high-recall) post-hoc; (4) AUPRC is robust to class imbalance. Success criterion: transformer AUPRC must exceed baseline AUPRC by ≥0.03 absolute points on the held-out test set.

**Secondary metrics**: AUC-ROC (sensitivity-specificity), precision and recall at fixed clinical thresholds (e.g., top 10%, top 20% of patients flagged as high-risk), calibration measures (expected calibration error, Brier score), and stratified performance by hospital site and service line (medical, surgical, etc.) to detect distribution shift and expose confounders.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

The validation set (Months 37–48) is used exclusively for hyperparameter selection, early stopping, and final model locking. Both the transformer and baseline are tuned to maximize AUPRC on this set. The test set (Months 49–60) is held out and untouched during all development and selection phases. After both models are finalized, the test set is evaluated exactly once, and results are reported with 95% confidence intervals (computed via bootstrap if test set size permits).

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Three known confounds are explicitly managed:
1. **Missingness patterns and record length**: Both models receive explicit missingness indicator features and sequence-length normalization, preventing one model from gaining unfair advantage from better imputation or length-aware encoding.
2. **Hospital coding practices**: Stratified evaluation by hospital site ensures that site-specific coding variations do not bias the comparison. If performance diverges markedly by site, this signals confounding rather than true model superiority.
3. **Service line effects**: Secondary metric reporting is broken down by service line (medical, surgical, etc.) to detect if the transformer gains advantage only on specific populations.

**Scope of claims**: This experiment establishes that the transformer outperforms the baseline for unplanned 30-day readmission prediction within this specific health system and during the study period. The design does not establish generalization to other health systems, different EHR systems, or cohorts with different case-mix or coding practices; external validation would be required for such claims.
